title: Calathea spicata
taxonomy: Calathea spicata is classified as a member of the family Marantaceae, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the prayer plant family. This classification is based on its morphological characteristics and genetic relationships with other plants in the same family. Calathea spicata is further grouped into the genus Calathea, which contains a wide variety of species with similar characteristics. The genus Calathea is divided into several subgenera, including Calathea, Phyllodes, and Goeppertia, to which Calathea spicata belongs. By classifying Calathea spicata under these categories, botanists can better understand its evolutionary history and relationships with other plants.

side_effects: Calathea spicata, also known as the Pinstripe Calathea, is a popular houseplant known for its beautiful, striped leaves. While it is generally considered non-toxic, consuming any part of the plant can cause oral and gastrointestinal irritation in humans and animals. In rare cases, ingesting large amounts of the plant may lead to more severe symptoms such as nausea and vomiting. If you have pets or children, it's essential to keep the plant out of reach to avoid potential side effects. intro: Calathea spicata, also known as the Purple Calathea or Pigtails, is a species of flowering plant in the family Marantaceae. This plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including the treatment of fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Calathea spicata include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Calathea spicata is commonly prepared as a tea or infusion, and is also used in the production of topical creams and ointments for wound healing and skin conditions.
